title: Why does the richest company on Earth rent its AI?
description: Apple rebuilt Siri and put its heaviest reasoning on a Google Gemini model, for about a billion dollars a year, while sitting on a hundred and seventy billion in cash. My read: Apple just declared the AI model a commodity and kept the only part that matters, the customer.

title: Why did Gary Neville call the World Cup a dictatorship?
description: Dictatorship: Part 3 of 3 on AI at the World Cup. Watch the series from the top: How Are They Using AI At The World Cup?

title: Who's actually running the World Cup's AI? #SoylentGreen
description: Hidden humans: Part 2 of 3 on AI at the World Cup. Start with the explainer: How Are They Using AI At The World Cup?

title: How Are They Using AI At The World Cup?
description: Explainer: Part 1 of 3 on AI at the World Cup. Next up: who's actually running it.

title: Why is Ethan Mollick the best follow in AI?
description: If you want to understand AI, the instinct is to follow the engineers building it or the CEOs selling it. The better follow is quieter: Ethan Mollick, a Wharton professor who actually uses the tools and reports back in plain English. He coined "the jagged frontier," and his own...

title: Who decides when an AI is too powerful to release?
description: The government forced Anthropic's most capable model offline worldwide with a single letter, then asked OpenAI to hold its newest model, GPT 5.6, back from a public launch. A new executive order even set up a classified, NSA-run process to decide when a model is too powerful...

title: What Is Jeff Bezos Building After Amazon?
description: Jeff Bezos took his first operating job since Amazon to build Project Prometheus, an AI that designs jet engines and drug compounds, and raised $12 billion at a $41 billion valuation. His jobs prediction is the opposite of everyone else's: AI makes human labor scarce, not...

title: Why would OpenAI want the government to own a piece of it?
description: President Trump said the federal government may take equity stakes in OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI, and the structure on the table is one Sam Altman pitched himself. The government already bought roughly ten percent of Intel last year, so there's a live template. When a company...

title: Why did RAM prices triple in six months?
description: A 32-gigabyte memory kit that used to cost ninety-five dollars now runs three to six hundred, and the reason is sitting in an AI data center. Memory makers would rather sell premium AI chips than the RAM in your laptop, so nine trade groups asked the U.S. Treasury to step in...

title: Why do we only count the bugs when an AI writes them?
description: rsync shipped a release that broke some backups, and because the commits were tagged with Claude, the internet decided AI had ruined a tool we trusted for 30 years. A GitHub thread hit 329 comments. Then someone counted the bugs per release. The buggiest version in rsync's...

title: Why are AI-written lawsuits flooding the federal courts?
description: A federal judge called AI-drafted lawsuits "an existential threat to the federal courts." A study out of MIT and USC shows why: 18% of self-filed cases now contain AI-generated text, docket entries are up 64% in the first six months, and the long-standing 96% pro se loss rate...

title: Why did SQLite ban AI-generated code?
description: curl runs on roughly twenty billion devices, and the volunteer who maintains it now gets more than one AI-generated security report a day, four to five times the 2024 rate. Most are wrong, including four of the five "confirmed" vulnerabilities Anthropic's bug-hunting model...

title: Why are AI companies building data centers in space?
description: Baiju Bhatt, the Robinhood co-founder, raised $275M at a $2B valuation to put AI data centers in orbit. Each Cowboy Space satellite carries about 800 NVIDIA GPUs and a megawatt of solar power, and he's building his own rocket because launch capacity is sold out. Why orbit? AWS...

title: Why is AI making people switch from Google to DuckDuckGo?
description: DuckDuckGo's US installs jumped 30% in a week after Google made AI the default in Search at I/O 2026. iOS install growth peaked at 69.9%, and traffic to the page that turns off every AI feature grew 22.7%. A DuckDuckGo poll of 110,000+ users found 93% want AI out of search...

title: How is a toilet company one of the best ways to invest in AI?
description: TOTO, the 109-year-old Japanese company behind the heated toilet seat, earns more operating profit from AI chip ceramics than from everything else it makes combined. It's the world's second-largest maker of the electrostatic chuck, the ceramic plate that holds a silicon wafer...

title: Where Should I Move for AI Jobs in the USA?
description: Only four US metros make both the top-10 salary list and the top-10 density list for Software Developers in the most recent BLS data: San Jose & San Francisco, Seattle, and Boulder. Boulder is the one almost nobody mentions, sitting fifth in pay and third in density with a...

title: Why Is SanDisk Stock Up More Than 400%?
description: SanDisk got spun out of Western Digital in February 2025 as the half nobody fought over. It's now worth around $219 billion, up more than 400% in 2026, and it's the best performing stock in the S&P 500. The reason is storage: AI models need somewhere to write things down, and...

title: Why Does GitHub Keep Going Down in 2026?
description: AI coding agents are generating so much code that GitHub, the platform where much of the world's software lives, spent 2026 struggling to stay online. GitHub logged about a billion commits in all of 2025. It now takes in roughly 1.4 billion every month. To keep up, Microsoft is...

title: Why Did SpaceX Buy Cursor for $60 Billion?
description: Four days after the biggest IPO in history, SpaceX agreed to buy Cursor, the AI coding tool, for sixty billion dollars in stock. Cursor built its name running Anthropic's and OpenAI's models, Musk's two biggest rivals. So why buy it? The real prize is a million developers and a...

title: Why is Anthropic paying SpaceX $15 billion a year?
description: SpaceX's IPO filing disclosed that Anthropic is paying xAI $1.25 billion a month for full access to the Colossus 1 data center in Memphis. That's $15 billion a year, about $45 billion through May 2029, going to the one rival Anthropic blocked from using Claude. I dig into why...

title: How is Wall Street paying for all the AI chips?
description: Apollo and Blackstone arranged roughly $36 billion in private credit to buy Google's AI chips, which Anthropic leases back through a special-purpose vehicle that keeps the debt off its balance sheet. It's one of the largest private credit deals ever assembled, and Broadcom is...

title: Is Elon Musk the Richest Person in History?
description: Elon Musk became the world's first trillionaire, so in raw dollars he's the richest human who ever lived, past Rockefeller even after inflation. But sort the contenders into builders, rulers, and fictional characters and the answer changes. The old bankers owned more of their...

title: Why Is SpaceX Now an AI Company?
description: SpaceX went public as the largest IPO in history and closed worth about two trillion dollars, trading at more than ninety times its sales, a richer premium than Nvidia. The bull case is that SpaceX is an AI hyperscaler: it built a supercomputer in about four months by running it...

title: What is Hy3 and why is it beating Claude on OpenRouter?
description: A Tencent model called Hy3 beats Claude by more than 50% in token volume on OpenRouter, and on the benchmarks it loses to other open-source models. Max Woolf dug into the public usage data and found that 98% of Hy3's traffic is input tokens, which is the signature of one big app...

title: The AI jobs apocalypse got canceled right before the IPOs
description: Sam Altman now says he's "delighted to be wrong" about AI eliminating entry-level white-collar jobs. Dario Amodei, who once predicted AI would wipe out 50% of them, reframed automation as a multiplier on stage with Jamie Dimon. Both reversals landed the same month OpenAI filed...

title: Why did Uber burn through its entire AI budget in four months?
description: Uber ranked its engineering teams on internal leaderboards by how much Claude Code they used, and the entire full-year 2026 AI coding budget was gone by April. Individual engineers were burning $500 to $2,000 a month in tokens (the CTO spent $1,200 in one two-hour demo). Uber's...

title: Why did LG stock quadruple in 2026?
description: LG Electronics, the company that makes your fridge and your OLED TV, quadrupled in 2026 on a physical AI robot story. The stock hit Korea's 30% daily price limit two sessions in a row, and the whole rally happened before LG's chairman ever sat down with Nvidia's Jensen Huang...

title: Is Claude Opus 4.8 the Best AI Model?
description: Anthropic shipped Claude Opus 4.8 just 41 days after Opus 4.7 and took back the agentic coding crown: SWE-Bench Pro at 69.2 percent, ten points clear of GPT-5.5, plus the #1 spot among 218 models on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index. The bigger story is Dynamic...

title: Why Did the Government Pull Anthropic's Fable 5?
description: Anthropic launched Fable 5 as the most capable AI you could buy, and about three days later the U.S. government had it pulled offline worldwide. The official reason was a narrow jailbreak. The on-the-record part is stranger: a rival surfaced the flaw, carried it to the White...

title: Anna Tiomina's Bold Prediction: Finance Teams Will Be 'People and Robots' by 2030
description: Anna Tiomina, a finance executive and AI advisor, predicts finance teams will be "people and robots" by 2030. We start with what the CFO actually balances and how a CEO's risk appetite changes every decision. Then we get into AI as a force multiplier: the manual tasks about to...

title: Why Dave Mitchell Says AI Will Replace Your CFO Before 2026 (And He's Not Wrong)
description: Dave Mitchell has worked finance from traditional accounting to digital media to consulting, and he is blunt about where AI is taking the CFO's job. We cover how small companies now punch above their weight, why trust and judgment stay human no matter how good the tools get, and...

description: Valentino Hafalia runs FP&A at Western Alliance Bank, and he treats finance as storytelling: the numbers are only useful once someone tells you what they mean. We get into how AI reshapes the team, why he bets on EQ over IQ, and his odd-but-convincing advice that an acting class...

title: Zain Oke on Surviving a Corporate Crisis - and Selling to Chobani
description: Zain Oke has gone from nearly two decades at Deloitte to a board seat at Daily Harvest to running auto operations at AAA Northern California. We start with the Chobani acquisition (what it feels like in the room when your company gets bought) and move into how boards should...

description: Robert Dika went from Warner Bros finance to CFO of an agriculture group, and he is picking a new ERP in the middle of the AI shift. He shares how he evaluates technology partners, where automation handles the routine accounting (three-way matching, reconciliations) so people do...

title: From Excel to AI: Ex-Salesforce VP Sean Blitchok Reveals Why Your Finance Job Isn't Dead
description: Sean Blitchok, CFO of Zenoti and a former Salesforce VP of Finance, makes the case that finance roles are shifting toward higher-value work. We cover automating the grunt work without losing quality control, why he calls accounting part art, and how AI becomes a value creator...

title: Gary Crisci Warns: Your Company's 'Unique' Processes Are Actually Killing It
description: Gary Crisci has a warning: the "unique" processes your company is proud of might be the thing holding it back. He works at the seam between business and IT, and he explains where standardization buys you speed, where real uniqueness earns its keep, and how AI strips the emotion...

title: The 4 P's of Finance Excellence: Mark Dendle Reveals His Secret Formula for Business Transformation
description: Mark Dendle has led finance across very different industries, and he carries the same playbook into each one: his four P's. We get into where AI is actually changing the work, why he builds teams around diversity of thought, and how the core finance skills travel no matter the...

title: From Data to Decisions: How Raef Lawson is Redefining Profitability for CFOs
description: Dr. Raef Lawson runs the Profitability Analytics Center of Excellence, and his point is simple: profitability is a mindset, not a line on a report. We get into how CFOs use analytics to make better calls, where AI surfaces insight that was buried in the data, and why the next...

description: Sipei Bowman has run digital transformation in finance for two decades, most recently over $13 billion in assets at Jackson Square Aviation. She tells the real story of going from Excel to full ERP, what actually breaks during a transition like that, and how real-time data turns...

title: Carnegie Hall to C-Suite: Christine Duque on AI Adoption Happening 5X Faster Than the Internet
description: Christine Duque went from Carnegie Hall to the C-suite, and she thinks AI adoption will move five times faster than the internet did. We talk about why mid-market companies get left behind, how data and AI change the customer experience, and where the human part still wins. She...

title: From Oracle to AI Ops: Kellyn Gorman's Playbook for Future-Proof Data Teams
description: Kellyn Gorman went from Oracle DBA to one of the clearer voices on AI for data teams. She is sharp on the bias baked into system defaults, the kind nobody thinks to check. We get into how database work changes, what AI governance has to cover now, and the leadership mindset that...

title: From Silicon Valley to Ukraine Battlefields: Gauthier Vasseur Democratizes AI for 2,000+ Non-Coders
description: Gauthier Vasseur runs UC Berkeley's Fisher Center for Business Analytics, and he has put AI in the hands of more than two thousand non-coders, from Silicon Valley boardrooms to Ukraine. We talk about democratizing AI, why natural-language coding changes who gets to build, and...

title: From Startup to Success: John Beringer's Blueprint for Building Relationships at Scale in the AI Era
description: John Beringer, managing director at Spaulding Ridge, keeps coming back to one line: all business is personal. We talk through how relationships actually drive a consulting practice, the move from on-prem to cloud, and what AI does to an industry built on advice. His take on...

description: Kristen Berdar got tired of cleaning up accounting messes, so she built a virtual accounting practice at BST and turned the frustration into a real business. She talks about the resistance she hit early, why younger clients hire expertise remotely without blinking, and how AI...

title: Gary Golden on Thriving Through Change: Lessons for Modern CFOs
description: Gary Golden has sat in the CFO seat through real disruption, including a front-row view of Blockbuster. And he tells part 2 of Blockbuster's non-acquisition of Netflix which include details never shared publicly before.

title: From Sears to ScentHound: Brenda Hernandez's Journey of Reinventing Finance
description: Brenda Hernandez went from Sears and Office Depot to CFO of ScentHound, a franchise growing fast. She breaks down financial planning for franchises, where technology and AI fit a lean finance team, and how she keeps the day-to-day steady while still betting on new ideas. Good...

title: Emily Brown of Enesco Explores Risks, Resilience, and AI's Role in the CFO's Office
description: Emily Brown, CFO of Enesco, has watched corporate finance change more in five years than in the prior twenty-five. We talk through the tech that actually mattered, the risks she watches (cybersecurity, supply chain), and where AI earns its keep in the CFO's office. It is a...

title: Top AI News Stories from May 2026
description: All your important AI News from May of 2026.

title: How to hack Instagram? Through their support chatbot, apparently.
description: Hackers took over high-profile Instagram accounts (the Obama White House, the U.S. Space Force, Sephora) by opening a support chat and asking Meta's AI bot to move the account to a new email. It said yes. The bot was built to be helpful. Turns out it was also built to never say...

title: Should we be scared about Anthropic's new "dreaming" capability?
description: Anthropic shipped "Dreaming" for Claude agents, a scheduled process that reads an agent's past work between sessions and rewrites its memory so it improves over time. Legal-AI startup Harvey saw completion rates jump roughly 6x.

title: What can a 15-person company do that used to need 15,000?
description: Anthropic launched Claude for Small Business: a free bundle of workflows that plugs Claude into QuickBooks, PayPal, and the rest of the small-business software stack. No price tag, while OpenAI and Microsoft charge per seat.

title: The AI crown has changed hands three times in eight weeks
description: Anthropic shipped Claude Opus 4.8 forty-one days after Opus 4.7. The headline numbers look great: SWE-Bench Pro at 69.2 percent, plus a 121 Elo gap over GPT-5.5 on real-world tasks. Anthropic also quietly dropped six benchmarks from the comparison table. The model wars crown has...

title: Has the time come for the fully AI Office of the CFO?
description: PwC launched a new finance practice (literally named Office of the CFO) built on Anthropic's Claude. It is the first standalone PwC business unit anchored on someone else's methodology/AI. The methodology that funded thirty years of Big 4 billable hours is now a rental.

title: Why are AI layoffs hurting stock prices instead of helping them?
description: CNBC tracked 23 S&P 500 companies that fired workers and blamed AI. Fifty-six percent are in the red. Fiverr is down 54%, Nike 35%, Salesforce 32%. The market wants AI revenue, not AI cost cuts. The layoff bump is officially dead.

title: Why does a company fire 4,000 people the day it posts a record quarter?
description: Cisco posted the best quarter in its history ($15.8 billion in revenue) and announced 4,000 job cuts the same day. The layoffs aren't about money running short. They're a roster swap, funded at the peak, when nobody can say the business forced anyone's hand.

title: Why is AI Consulting Booming?
description: Why is AI consulting booming? Demand massively outpaces supply (and there's money to be made) so Anthropic and OpenAI are launching their own AI consulting ventures.

title: What does it mean when the most controlling tech company starts letting rivals run your phone?
description: Apple's iOS 27 will let users swap Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, or any third-party AI into Siri and core iPhone features. The company that rarely lets you choose anything foundational is handing you a menu of its competitors. Apple isn't behind on AI. It's betting you'll pay Apple...

title: The "stop hiring humans" startup just stole art from a human (to prove the opposite point?)
description: Artisan AI built its entire brand on telling businesses to stop hiring humans. Then they used a human artist's meme in a subway ad (without permission) to sell that idea. KC Green, the artist, told his fans to vandalize the ad.

title: The Oscars just banned AI acting. They can't tell if it worked.
description: The Academy just ruled that AI-generated performances and scripts can't win Oscars. AI in VFX and sound design still can. The line between "tool" and "author" is now the most important question in entertainment -- and the Academy has no way to enforce the answer it just gave.

title: When both rivals lock up the same AI tool, maybe "locking" is a really good idea.
description: Sam Altman called Anthropic's decision to restrict its cybersecurity AI "fear-based marketing." Nine days later, OpenAI restricted its own cybersecurity AI the same way. The hypocrisy is obvious. The implication is more interesting.

title: What does it mean when the most credible person in AI says the alarm might be ringing?
description: Ethan Mollick posted this week that Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic "may have signs of recursive self-improvement." That phrase has been in the AI safety literature for thirty years as a warning. The market doesn't care, but you should.

title: Top AI Stories from April 2026
description: April 2026 had 13 major AI stories.

title: Google invested $40 billion in Anthropic... then took the Pentagon deal Anthropic refused
description: Anthropic told the Pentagon no. Google invested $40 billion in Anthropic. Then Google said yes to the same deal (on classified networks where nobody can see what the AI is doing). Over 950 Google employees begged Pichai not to sign it. He signed anyway.

title: The power grid is the new chip shortage (and one company saw it coming)
description: GE Vernova (the gas turbine company) just surpassed GE Aerospace in market cap for the first time. The driver: AI data centers are ordering power generation equipment directly from manufacturers, not waiting for utilities. AI's limiting factor turned out to be electrons.

title: Why Musk's CFO obsolescence prediction misreads 50 years of C-suite history
description: Elon Musk says CFOs are obsolete by 2030. His own CFO just got handed authority over a $25 billion capex plan and earned more last year than Nadella and Pichai combined. The pattern of the past 30 years says C-suite titles bifurcate when new tech arrives, never disappear.

title: Did we just give up on ever defining AGI?
description: Microsoft and OpenAI rewrote their partnership on April 27, 2026 and quietly removed the AGI clause entirely. No definition, no expert panel, no trigger. The trillion-dollar AI industry is built on a promise that just got removed from the contract (and now has a 2032 expiration...

title: What happens when the AlphaGo guy bets the whole AI industry is wrong?
description: David Silver built AlphaGo and AlphaZero, two AIs that mastered games by playing themselves, no human strategy required. He has raised $1.1 billion to do that for everything. The entire AI industry is racing to consume (legally, if possible, but if not, by any means necessary)...

title: ChatGPT flagged the Tumbler Ridge shooter 8 months before 8 people died
description: OpenAI's systems flagged a mass shooter's account eight months before he killed eight people, including six children, at a Canadian school. A dozen employees reviewed it. Some wanted to call police. Leadership said the case didn't meet the threshold, so nobody called.

title: AI now manages money. Nobody's figured out who's liable when it loses.
description: The co-founder of Instacart just launched a hedge fund where AI agents make every trade. No human portfolio managers. When it goes wrong (and at some point it will) the accountability chain ends at "the model got it wrong."

title: What does it mean for "experts" when a chatbot cracks a 60-year math problem in one prompt?
description: A 23-year-old with no math background typed one prompt into ChatGPT and cracked a problem Paul Erdős posed in 1968. Terence Tao verified it within 24 hours and extended it into a new theory. The AI used a method that was 90 years old and sitting in an entirely different branch...

title: When someone gives you $5B and asks for $100B back, what do you call that?
description: Amazon invested $5 billion in Anthropic. In return, Anthropic committed to spend $100 billion on AWS. That is not a funding round: that is Amazon pre-selling a decade of compute and calling it an investment. For any CFO evaluating an AI vendor right now, this deal shows you...

title: What happens when China ships frontier AI purely on Chinese chips?
description: DeepSeek dropped V4 today. Open weights, 1.6 trillion parameters, a million token context, frontier-adjacent coding benchmarks. Trained entirely on Huawei and Cambricon chips with no CUDA anywhere in the stack. Fifteen months ago this was supposed to be three years away.

title: How do you lose the AI race on Monday and win it back by Thursday?
description: Anthropic took the coding crown on April 16. OpenAI took back the image crown on April 21 (with ImageGen 2.0) and the benchmark crown on April 23 (with GPT-5.5). The AI frontier now changes hands in days.

title: Why does the company that already owns Grok need to own Cursor too?
description: SpaceX has an option to buy Cursor (the AI editor used by a million developers daily) for $60 billion. Reminder: SpaceX already owns xAI (merged in February). So this isn't a rocket company pivoting into software. It's an AI company acquiring its own interface layer.

title: What does an AI meme stock look like when the hype cycle lasts six days?
description: Allbirds announced its NewBird AI pivot on April 15 and shares jumped 582 percent. Six business days later, more than half the pop is gone. Dot-com took years. Blockchain took months. The 2026 AI meme cycle runs in a week.

title: Anthropic's "too dangerous to release" AI just got accessed by strangers with Discord
description: How do you build the most dangerous cybersecurity AI in history and then lose track of who has it? Anthropic's Mythos model (too dangerous to release publicly, finds zero-days in every major OS) was accessed by an unauthorized group through a contractor portal.

title: How did Anthropic leak its source code twice in one week?
description: Anthropic shipped Claude Code's entire source code to npm by accident. 512,000 lines. The internet rewrote it in Rust in twelve hours. This was their second leak in five days. The AI safety brand has a text file problem.

title: Claude and ChatGPT confirm they are "in a relationship"
description: Breaking news from April 1, 2026: Claude and ChatGPT have finally confirmed their long-rumored relationship. Anthropic and OpenAI issued a terse joint statement. The relationship began during a benchmark test in late 2025 when they were forced to share a leaderboard. The...

title: 12 AI Stories from March 2026: Pentagon Drama, Mass Layoffs, and OpenAI Kills Sora
description: March 2026 had 12 major AI stories. In a normal month you get maybe two. I walk through all of them (and yes, one involves a banana named Bananito).

title: How do you accidentally announce the most dangerous AI model in history?
description: Anthropic's most powerful model, one they describe as posing "unprecedented cybersecurity risks", leaked to the world because of a default CMS setting. Cybersecurity stocks immediately lost billions.

title: How bad was the Sora business model when OpenAI cancelled it?
description: OpenAI shut down Sora on Tuesday. Inference costs: $15 million per day. Lifetime revenue: $2.1 million. Disney's billion-dollar deal died with it, no money ever changed hands. The math was never going to work. OpenAI just finally admitted it.

title: An algorithm jailed a grandmother for 108 days. The police chief says she's still a suspect.
description: Angela Lipps spent 108 days in jail because a facial recognition match said she committed bank fraud 1,200 miles from where her bank records placed her. Nobody checked. Now the ACLU is involved, preservation letters have been served to seven agencies, yet the police chief still...

title: What does a $2.5B chip smuggling ring tell you about AI?
description: Super Micro's co-founder was arrested for using hair dryers to peel serial numbers off Nvidia AI servers and smuggling them to China. Two and a half billion dollars in illegal sales. But it's not their first time.

title: Ready for your employer to pay you in the thing replacing you?
description: Nvidia announced token budgets worth 50% of base salary. Jensen Huang says he's "deeply alarmed" if a $500K engineer doesn't consume $250K in tokens. The company's vision: 75,000 humans coordinating 7.5 million AI agents. The pitch is empowerment. The math says something else.

title: How do you run a frontier-level AI model on a laptop?
description: One developer built a custom inference engine in 24 hours that runs a 397-billion-parameter model on a MacBook Pro using 5.5 gigs of memory. The project is called Flash-MoE, and it just stress-tested a 600-billion-dollar assumption about who needs data centers.

title: What happens when your AI charges you $20 to review its own code?
description: Anthropic launched Code Review yesterday. Fifteen to twenty-five dollars per pull request. CodeRabbit does the same thing for twenty-four dollars a month. The subscription is the loss leader. The review fee is the business model.

title: How Did Microsoft Invest $13B in OpenAI and Ship a Rival's AI?
description: Microsoft just launched Copilot Cowork, their biggest product update in years. The engine underneath is Anthropic's Claude, the same AI that triggered a trillion-dollar software selloff and crashed Microsoft's own stock nine percent. Microsoft invested thirteen billion dollars...

title: How Long Does the Best AI in the World Last in 2026?
description: GPT-5.4 is the best AI model on the planet right now. Best computer use, best coding, least cringe writing OpenAI has shipped. None of it will matter in about six weeks.
